perturbation.original_text: Meetings of the JSC may be called by either Party on no less than thirty (30) days' notice during the Term.
perturbation.changed_text: Meetings of the JSC may be called by either Party on no less than ten (10) days' notice during the Term.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for JSC meetings to ten days could be problematic if significant preparation is required by committee members. This contradicts general principles of contract law that require adequate time for parties to fulfill obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UCC section emphasizes the importance of reasonableness and industry standards in contractual dealings, implying that parties should have sufficient time to prepare for meetings and fulfill obligations. Reducing the notice period could be seen as unreasonable, especially if complex issues are discussed, potentially rendering decisions made at such meetings challengeable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-205. Reasonable time; Seasonableness. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-205. Reasonable time; Seasonableness. (a) Whether a time for taking an action required by the Uniform Commercial Code is reasonable depends on the nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action. (b) An action is taken seasonably if it is taken at or within the time agreed or, if no time is agreed, at or within a reasonable time. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for JSC meetings from thirty (30) days to ten (10) days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could be problematic if committee members require significant preparation time. The Law Explanation suggests that reducing the notice period could be seen as unreasonable, potentially rendering decisions made at such meetings challengeable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, UCC § 1-205, defines 'reasonable time' as depending on the 'nature, purpose, and circumstances of the action' and seasonableness as 'at or within the time agreed or, if no time is agreed, at or within a reasonable time.' A reduction from 30 days to 10 days may be deemed unreasonable depending on the complexity of the issues to be addressed at the JSC meeting. Therefore, the change introduces a potential contradiction by shortening the period and potentially undermining the 'reasonable time' requirement of the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: Valeant shall keep accurate and complete records, consistent with pharmaceutical industry standards, of each Detail and its obligations hereunder in connection therewith. Such records shall be kept for the longer of (i) [***] after the end of the Calendar Year to which they relate and (ii) such period of time as required by Applicable Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: Valeant shall keep accurate and complete records, consistent with pharmaceutical industry standards, of each Detail and its obligations hereunder in connection therewith. Such records shall be kept for two (2) years after the end of the Calendar Year to which they relate, regardless of requirements from the Applicable Laws.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record-keeping period to two years contradicts federal regulations that mandate longer retention periods for certain pharmaceutical records.
perturbation.law_explanation: 21 CFR § 211.180 requires that records be maintained for at least one year after the expiration date of the batch. In many instances, this exceeds two years, so the modified text creates non-compliance with FDA regulations and could result in penalties.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: base your decision on whether to apply audit trails, or other appropriate measures, on the need to comply with predicate rule requirements, a justified and documented risk assessment, and a determination of the potential effect on product quality and safety and record integrity. We suggest that you apply appropriate controls based on such an assessment. Audit trails can be particularly appropriate when users are expected to create, modify, or delete regulated records during normal operation. Legacy Systems 7 The Agency intends to exercise enforcement discretion with respect to all part 11 requirements for systems that otherwise were operational prior to August 20, 1997, the effective date of part 11, under the circumstances specified below. This means that the Agency does not intend to take enforcement action to enforce compliance with any part 11 requirements if all the following criteria are met for a specific system: The system was operational before the effective date. The system met all applicable predicate rule requirements before the effective date. The system currently meets all applicable predicate rule requirements. You have documented evidence and justification that the system is fit for its intended use (including having an acceptable level of record security and integrity, if applicable). If a system has been changed since August 20, 1997, and if the changes would prevent the system from meeting predicate rule requirements, Part 11 controls should be applied to Part 11 records and signatures pursuant to the enforcement policy expressed in this guidance. Copies of Records The Agency intends to exercise enforcement discretion with regard to specific part 11 requirements for generating copies of records (§ 11.10 (b) and any corresponding requirement in §11.30). You should provide an investigator with reasonable and useful access to records during an inspection. All records held by you are subject to inspection in accordance with predicate rules (e.g., §§ 211.180(c), (d), and 108.35(c)(3)(ii)). We recommend that you supply copies of electronic records by: Producing copies of records held in common portable formats when records are maintained in these formats Using established automated conversion or export methods, where available, to make copies in a more common format (examples of such formats include, but are not limited to, PDF, XML, or SGML) In each case, we recommend that the copying process used produces copies that preserve the content and meaning of the record. If you have the ability to search, sort, or trend part 11 records, copies given to the Agency should provide the same capability if it is reasonable and technically feasible. You should allow inspection, review, and copying of records in a human readable form at your site using your hardware and following your established procedures and techniques for accessing records. Record Retention The Agency intends to exercise enforcement discretion with regard to the part 11 requirements for the protection of records to enable their accurate and ready retrieval throughout the records retention period (§ 11.10 (c) and any corresponding requirement in §11.30). Persons must still comply with all applicable predicate rule requirements for record retention and availability (e.g., §§ 211.180(c),(d), 108.25(g), and 108.35(h)). We suggest that your decision on how to maintain records be based on predicate rule requirements and that you base your decision on a justified and documented risk assessment and a determination of the value of the records over time. FDA does not intend to object if you decide to archive required records in electronic format to nonelectronic media such as microfilm, microfiche, and paper, or to a standard electronic file format (examples of such formats include, but are not limited to, PDF, XML, or SGML). Persons must still comply with all predicate rule requirements, and the records themselves and any copies of the required records should preserve th
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the record-keeping period to two years, regardless of what Applicable Laws require. The Law Explanation states that 21 CFR § 211.180 requires records to be maintained for at least one year after the expiration date of the batch, which can exceed two years. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions §§ 211.180(c), (d) in relation to record retention and availability, suggesting that the original text's compliance with 'Applicable Laws' is important, and the change directly contradicts this by setting a fixed, shorter period, creating non-compliance.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of [***] thereafter, it shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement (which includes the exercise of its rights or performance of any obligations hereunder) any Confidential Information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other Party pursuant to this Agreement, except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that, during the Term and for a period of one (1) year thereafter, it shall keep confidential and shall not publish or otherwise disclose and shall not use for any purpose other than as provided for in this Agreement (which includes the exercise of its rights or performance of any obligations hereunder) any Confidential Information furnished to it by or on behalf of the other Party pursuant to this Agreement, except to the extent expressly authorized by this Agreement or otherwise agreed in writing by the Parties.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year introduces uncertainty and may fail to adequately protect confidential business information, because trade secret laws require longer protection periods.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secret Laws - Duration of Protection
perturbation.law_explanation: Trade secret laws, like the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), protect trade secrets for as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect certain trade secrets, potentially conflicting with the protections intended by such laws and exposing a party to legal action for misappropriation even after the end of the contractual confidentiality period.
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period to one year, potentially conflicting with trade secret laws like the DTSA, as trade secrets are protected as long as they remain confidential and provide a competitive advantage. The Economic Espionage Act of 1996, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, underscores the importance of protecting trade secrets, and a one-year limit may be insufficient, exposing a party to misappropriation claims even after the confidentiality period ends. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this may fail to adequately protect confidential business information, because trade secret laws require longer protection periods.
